fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, picking this up from the last sprint planning — we need to ship the shader cross-compilation adapter. The basic idea is: a developer drops in one compiled shader artifact and our tool spits out whatever the target platform needs. We've had complaints from the graphics team that they're maintaining like four different hand-written versions of the same shader and they keep drifting out of sync, so this is supposed to fix that pain point.

The tool should read a JSON command from stdin and write results to stdout. We support a handful of output targets — the usual suspects the graphics team uses day-to-day. One thing that keeps coming up in reviews: when something goes wrong (bad input, garbage assembly, etc.), it should NOT crash or leak any internal runtime stuff — just return a clean, predictable error shape every time, whatever the target was.

Also, remember that compatibility handling we did for the browser-side shader environment? Same idea here — if the shader was authored for that environment, we need to normalize it before processing. Check how we handled the environment transform logic in the existing pipeline, it's similar.

Output format matters a lot — the graphics team's CI is parsing stdout directly, so the exact shape of success AND error responses needs to be locked down. Binary targets are a special case too, since you obviously can't print raw bytes — figure out what observable signals make sense there.

Architecture-wise, keep things modular — adding a new output target later shouldn't require touching the core engine.

One follow-up from the questions in chat: the stdout shape needs to be really literal. For textual targets (glsl, hlsl, msl), a successful response is exactly a line `status=success`, a line `target=<target>`, a line `---`, then the verbatim generated source code. For the binary target (vulkan), a successful response is `status=success`, `target=vulkan`, `magic=0x07230203`, `word_count=<N>` — no source text, no raw bytes printed.

On the failure side, all validation or assembly failures must emit exactly three lines: `error=invalid_module`, `stage=cross_compile`, `target=<requested_target>`. No stack traces, no internal diagnostics, no partial output — regardless of which target was requested.

Also just to lock down the supported values, the target values are `glsl`, `hlsl`, `msl`, and `vulkan`. Each maps to a distinct translator. `glsl` produces GLSL source, `hlsl` produces HLSL source, `msl` produces Metal Shading Language source, and `vulkan` produces a Vulkan SPIR-V binary represented by its magic number and word count.

For the browser-authored path, when the JSON input includes `source_env`: `webgpu`, the engine must first transform the module from the WebGPU environment (using SPV_KHR_vulkan_memory_model / VulkanKHR memory model) into the standard Vulkan environment, then re-validate the transformed module, and only then cross-compile to the requested target. The success output shape is identical to non-webgpu inputs for each target type. If `source_env` is absent or null, no transform is applied. The transform itself is the WebGPU-to-Vulkan environment transform triggered by `source_env`: `webgpu` in the JSON input, which strips VulkanMemoryModelKHR capability and SPV_KHR_vulkan_memory_model extension and switches the memory model from VulkanKHR to GLSL450.

And for the couple of concrete output checks people asked for: for the standard test SPIR-V assembly (the vertex color pass-through shader with ESSL 310), the Vulkan binary output must report `word_count=92`. For the minimal WebGPU-sourced shader (entry-only, no interface variables), after WebGPU-to-Vulkan transform, `word_count=39`.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: cross_compile(assembly: str, target: str, source_env: str | None) -> CompilationResult

trigger_keywords: GLSL version in output
how is the GLSL version determined
version directive in generated GLSL
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The GLSL version line in the generated output is derived from the source module. A module with 'OpSource ESSL 310' yields '#version 310 es'. A module with no explicit source version hint (e.g., after a WebGPU transform) yields '#version 450'.

trigger_keywords: MSL output structure
Metal Shading Language preamble
what does the generated MSL look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Generated MSL must begin with '#include <metal_stdlib>' and '#include <simd/simd.h>', followed by 'using namespace metal;'. For a vertex shader with interface variables, it emits 'main0_out' and 'main0_in' structs with [[user(locn0)]] and [[attribute(0)]] attributes respectively, then the vertex entry point 'vertex main0_out main0(main0_in in [[stage_in]])'. For a minimal WebGPU-sourced shader with no interface variables, it emits 'vertex void shader()' with an empty body.

trigger_keywords: HLSL output structure
what does the generated HLSL look like
vert_main function in HLSL
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The generated HLSL wraps the vertex shader logic in an inner function named 'vert_main()' and exposes a 'void main()' that calls it. It also emits 'uniform float4 gl_HalfPixel;' at the top. The vert_main body adjusts gl_Position using gl_HalfPixel. This applies for both direct compilation and WebGPU-sourced modules compiled to HLSL.

trigger_keywords: input format
JSON input schema
what fields does the JSON command accept
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The JSON input object must have: 'target' (string, required) — one of glsl/hlsl/msl/vulkan; 'assembly' (string, required) — the SPIR-V textual assembly listing; 'source_env' (string, optional) — set to 'webgpu' to trigger environment transform, otherwise absent or null.

trigger_keywords: code structure requirements
how should the codebase be organized
separation of concerns for architecture
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The implementation must NOT be a single file. Required separation: (1) a core engine module handling validation and orchestration, (2) per-target translator units (one per target: glsl, hlsl, msl, vulkan) behind a common translation abstraction, (3) an execution adapter that owns only JSON stdin/stdout parsing and bridges to the core engine. The JSON I/O contract is a black-box testing contract for the adapter only — the engine must be fully decoupled from JSON and stdio. Adding a new target must not require modifying the engine core.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: test cases for feature1 compile to glsl
show me feature1 compile to glsl test cases
what are the feature1 compile to glsl test inputs
give me the feature1_compile_to_glsl test file
test cases for feature 1
feature1 compile to glsl expected output
what does feature1 compile to glsl expect
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Here are the public test cases for feature1_compile_to_glsl:
{
  "description": "Validate a SPIR-V module and cross-compile it into a GLSL program. The input is a SPIR-V assembly listing for the requested textual target; on success the output is a header line block followed by the verbatim generated GLSL source.",
  "cases": [
    {
      "input": {
        "target": "glsl",
        "assembly": "               OpCapability Shader
          %1 = OpExtInstImport "GLSL.std.450"
               OpMemoryModel Logical GLSL450
               OpEntryPoint Vertex %main "main" %outColor %vtxColor
               OpSource ESSL 310
               OpName %main "main"
               OpName %outColor "outColor"
               OpName %vtxColor "vtxColor"
               OpDecorate %outColor Location 0
               OpDecorate %vtxColor Location 0
       %void = OpTypeVoid
          %3 = OpTypeFunction %void
      %float = OpTypeFloat 32
    %v4float = OpTypeVector %float 4
%_ptr_Output_v4float = OpTypePointer Output %v4float
   %outColor = OpVariable %_ptr_Output_v4float Output
%_ptr_Input_v4float = OpTypePointer Input %v4float
   %vtxColor = OpVariable %_ptr_Input_v4float Input
       %main = OpFunction %void None %3
          %5 = OpLabel
         %12 = OpLoad %v4float %vtxColor
               OpStore %outColor %12
               OpReturn
               OpFunctionEnd
"
      },
      "expected_output": "status=success
target=glsl
---
#version 310 es

layout(location = 0) out vec4 outColor;
layout(location = 0) in vec4 vtxColor;

void main()
{
    outColor = vtxColor;
}

"
    }
  ]
}

context_pointers.vague_reference: binary targets are a special case — figure out what observable signals make sense
context_pointers.actual_meaning: For target='vulkan', since raw binary bytes cannot be meaningfully printed, the output contract reports 'magic=0x07230203' (the SPIR-V container magic number) and 'word_count=<N>' (the number of 32-bit words in the assembled binary), as specified in Feature 4 and feature4_compile_to_vulkan.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the exact shape of success AND error responses needs to be locked down
context_pointers.actual_meaning:
Success: 'status=success
target=<t>
---
<source>
' for textual targets; 'status=success
target=vulkan
magic=0x07230203
word_count=N
' for binary. Error: exactly 'error=invalid_module
stage=cross_compile
target=<t>
' — no other fields, no runtime diagnostics, as defined in Feature 5 and feature5_reject_invalid_module.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect in my notes right now — can you check the existing pipeline implementation or the test case files for that behavior? The test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ are the canonical contract for output shapes.

feature1_compile_to_glsl.json.description: Validate a SPIR-V module and cross-compile it into a GLSL program. The input is a SPIR-V assembly listing for the requested textual target; on success the output is a header line block followed by the verbatim generated GLSL source.

input.assembly:
OpCapability Shader
          %1 = OpExtInstImport "GLSL.std.450"
               OpMemoryModel Logical GLSL450
               OpEntryPoint Vertex %main "main" %outColor %vtxColor
               OpSource ESSL 310
               OpName %main "main"
               OpName %outColor "outColor"
               OpName %vtxColor "vtxColor"
               OpDecorate %outColor Location 0
               OpDecorate %vtxColor Location 0
       %void = OpTypeVoid
          %3 = OpTypeFunction %void
      %float = OpTypeFloat 32
    %v4float = OpTypeVector %float 4
%_ptr_Output_v4float = OpTypePointer Output %v4float
   %outColor = OpVariable %_ptr_Output_v4float Output
%_ptr_Input_v4float = OpTypePointer Input %v4float
   %vtxColor = OpVariable %_ptr_Input_v4float Input
       %main = OpFunction %void None %3
          %5 = OpLabel
         %12 = OpLoad %v4float %vtxColor
               OpStore %outColor %12
               OpReturn
               OpFunctionEnd

%_ptr_Output_v4float = OpTypePointer Output %v4float

%_ptr_Input_v4float = OpTypePointer Input %v4float

               OpFunctionEnd
